div.p: Input flags = 0x00080241 O_ACCMODE = 0x3 (bits 0–1) O_WRONLY = 0x1 O_RDWR = 0x2 O_CREAT = 0x40 O_TRUNC = 0x200 O_CLOEXEC = 0x80000

Why O_RDONLY needs pre-extraction O_RDONLY = 0 → flags & 0 == 0 is always true. Generic decode_flags() would spuriously match every flags value as O_RDONLY. Solution: extract 2-bit access mode field first, then run generic loop on remaining bits.

access_mode = flags & O_ACCMODE = 0x00080241 & 0x3 = 0x1 → O_WRONLY Switch on access_mode: • 0 → print O_RDONLY • 1 → print O_WRONLY ✓ • 2 → print O_RDWR • else → print 0x%x Output so far: O_WRONLY first = 0 (have printed one token)
rest = flags & ~O_ACCMODE = 0x00080241 & ~0x3 = 0x00080241 & 0xFFFFFFFC = 0x00080240 Bits 0–1 cleared. Remaining bits represent only creation/status flags — safe for the generic bit-test loop.
if (rest != 0): print "|0x%lx" of rest rest = 0x00000000 → no residual If unknown flags were present, e.g. rest = 0x1000, output would append |0x1000.
Final Output O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC|O_CLOEXEC Complete symbolic representation. All bits accounted for. rest = 0.

O_RDONLY = 0 Special Case If flags = 0x00 (pure read-only): access_mode = 0 & 0x3 = 0 → print O_RDONLY rest = 0 & ~0x3 = 0 Loop: no entry has 0 & v == v for any v > 0. Output: O_RDONLY Without pre-extraction: (0 & 0) == 0 would match every zero-value entry in a generic bitmask loop.
Entry: O_CREAT = 0x40 Test: rest & 0x40 == 0x40? 0x00080240 & 0x40 = 0x40 ✓ MATCH → print |O_CREAT → rest &= ~0x40 → rest = 0x00080200
Entry: O_EXCL = 0x80 Test: rest & 0x80 == 0x80? 0x00080200 & 0x80 = 0x0 ✗ no match
Entry: O_NOCTTY = 0x400 Test: rest & 0x400 == 0x400? 0x00080200 & 0x400 = 0x0 ✗ no match
Entry: O_TRUNC = 0x200 Test: rest & 0x200 == 0x200? 0x00080200 & 0x200 = 0x200 ✓ MATCH → print |O_TRUNC → rest &= ~0x200 → rest = 0x00080000
Entry: O_APPEND = 0x400... All: rest & value = 0 ✗ no match
Entry: O_CLOEXEC = 0x80000 Test: rest & 0x80000 == 0x80000? 0x00080000 & 0x80000 = 0x80000 ✓ MATCH → print |O_CLOEXEC → rest &= ~0x80000 → rest = 0x00000000
